verses.text: And Solomon the son of David was strengthened in his kingdom, and the LORD his God was with him, and magnified him exceedingly. Then Solomon spake unto all Israel, to the captains of thousands and of hundreds, and to the judges, and to every governor in all Israel, the chief of the fathers. So Solomon, and all the congregation with him, went to the high place that was at Gibeon; for there was the tabernacle of the congregation of God, which Moses the servant of the LORD had made in the wilderness. But the ark of God had David brought up from Kirjathjearim to the place which David had prepared for it: for he had pitched a tent for it at Jerusalem. Moreover the brasen altar, that Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, had made, he put before the tabernacle of the LORD: and Solomon and the congregation sought unto it. And Solomon went up thither to the brasen altar before the LORD, which was at the tabernacle of the congregation, and offered a thousand burnt offerings upon it. Guidance: The second book of Chronicles opens with Solomon, now firmly established — 'the LORD his God was with him, and magnified him exceedingly' — leading all Israel in worship at Gibeon, where the ancient tabernacle of Moses and Bezaleel's brazen altar still stood. There Solomon 'offered a thousand burnt offerings.' The chapter is showing us a king who begins his reign not with conquest or display but with worship; before he asks anything of God, he gives himself to God in lavish sacrifice. The chapter is teaching us the secret of Solomon's strength and greatness: 'the LORD his God was with him.' As with David, so with Solomon — the presence of God was the source of all his magnifying. The chapter is teaching us the wisdom of beginning every undertaking, and every reign and season of life, with the worship of God; Solomon sought the LORD first, gathering all Israel to seek Him together. The chapter is teaching us the costliness of his devotion — a thousand burnt offerings, a king's extravagant gift of worship, holding nothing back from God at the threshold of his reign. The chapter is teaching us that those whom God would use greatly are those who first seek Him greatly; Solomon's wisdom and wealth, which the chapter will soon recount, flowed from a heart that began by worshiping. The chapter is teaching us to put God first in all things, to seek Him at the outset of every new chapter, and to give Him our best in worship — for the LORD honours those who honour Him, and the reign that begins at the altar begins well. Let us, like Solomon, seek the LORD before we seek anything else, and find that He is with us and able to magnify us exceedingly for His purposes and glory.

In that night did God appear unto Solomon, and said unto him, Ask what I shall give thee. And Solomon said unto God, Thou hast shewed great mercy unto David my father, and hast made me to reign in his stead. Now, O LORD God, let thy promise unto David my father be established: for thou hast made me king over a people like the dust of the earth in multitude. Give me now wisdom and knowledge, that I may go out and come in before this people: for who can judge this thy people, that is so great? Guidance: That night God appears to Solomon with a breathtaking offer: 'Ask what I shall give thee.' And Solomon's response reveals a heart rightly ordered. He does not seize the chance for personal gain but, mindful of God's mercy to David and the greatness of the people he must govern, asks: 'Give me now wisdom and knowledge, that I may go out and come in before this people: for who can judge this thy people, that is so great?' The chapter is showing us a young king who, given a blank check from God, asks not for himself but for what he needs to serve God's people well. The chapter is teaching us what to value most and to ask for first: wisdom and knowledge to do the work God has given us. Solomon felt the weight of his calling — 'who can judge this thy people?' — and knew his own insufficiency, and so he sought from God the wisdom to lead. The chapter is teaching us the humility that knows its need: Solomon did not presume he was equal to the task, but confessed he needed God's wisdom to govern a people so great. The chapter is teaching us to ground our requests in God's faithfulness and purposes, as Solomon did — 'let thy promise unto David my father be established' — asking for what will fulfill God's plan, not merely our own desires. The chapter is teaching us that the best prayers are those that ask for the means to serve God and others rather than for our own ease, riches, or advancement. James tells us, 'If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally' (James 1:5); Solomon did just this, and we are invited to do the same. The chapter is teaching us to feel the weight of our responsibilities, to confess our need, and to ask God for the wisdom and knowledge to fulfill the calling He has given us — for this is a request that delights the heart of God.

And God said to Solomon, Because this was in thine heart, and thou hast not asked riches, wealth, or honour, nor the life of thine enemies, neither yet hast asked long life; but hast asked wisdom and knowledge for thyself, that thou mayest judge my people, over whom I have made thee king: Wisdom and knowledge is granted unto thee; and I will give thee riches, and wealth, and honour, such as none of the kings have had that have been before thee, neither shall there any after thee have the like. Guidance: God is delighted with Solomon's request, and His answer is overflowing: 'Because this was in thine heart, and thou hast not asked riches, wealth, or honour... but hast asked wisdom and knowledge... Wisdom and knowledge is granted unto thee; and I will give thee riches, and wealth, and honour, such as none of the kings have had.' Solomon asked for one good thing and received it — plus all the things he had not asked for. The chapter is showing us the generosity of God toward those who seek the right things first; Solomon sought wisdom to serve, and God gave him wisdom and wealth and honour beyond all kings. The chapter is teaching us the great principle our Lord would later state plainly: 'Seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you' (Matt 6:33). Solomon sought first the wisdom to do God's work, and the lesser things — riches, honour — were added to him. The chapter is teaching us that God notices the heart behind our requests: 'Because this was in thine heart' — it was the unselfishness of Solomon's desire that pleased God. When our hearts are set on serving Him rather than ourselves, He delights to bless us. The chapter is teaching us that we lose nothing by putting God and His work first; far from it, we gain both the thing we sought and more besides. Those who grasp at riches and honour for their own sake often miss the wisdom they truly need; but those who seek wisdom to serve God often find that He adds the very things they did not ask for. The chapter is teaching us to order our desires rightly — to want most what most pleases God and most serves His people — and to trust that the God who is no man's debtor will give liberally, often far beyond what we asked or thought. Seek the right thing first, and leave the rest in the hands of a generous God.

Then Solomon came from his journey to the high place that was at Gibeon to Jerusalem, from before the tabernacle of the congregation, and reigned over Israel. And Solomon gathered chariots and horsemen: and he had a thousand and four hundred chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen, which he placed in the chariot cities, and with the king at Jerusalem. And the king made silver and gold at Jerusalem as plenteous as stones, and cedar trees made he as the sycomore trees that are in the vale for abundance. And Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt, and linen yarn: the king's merchants received the linen yarn at a price. And they brought up, and brought forth out of Egypt a chariot for six hundred shekels of silver, and an horse for an hundred and fifty: and so brought they out for all the kings of the Hittites, and for the kings of Syria, by their means. Guidance: God's promise is swiftly fulfilled: Solomon returns from Gibeon and reigns in such prosperity that he gathers great wealth — fourteen hundred chariots, twelve thousand horsemen, and 'silver and gold at Jerusalem as plenteous as stones,' cedar as common as the sycamore. The riches God promised flow in abundance. The chapter is showing us the LORD making good His word; having promised Solomon wealth and honour beyond all kings, He pours it out, so that gold becomes as common as stone in Jerusalem. The chapter is teaching us that God is faithful to His promises and able to bless beyond measure those whom He favours; what He pledges, He performs, and lavishly. Yet the discerning reader notes a quiet shadow in these verses: 'Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt,' multiplying chariots and horses — the very things the LORD had cautioned Israel's king against, lest he trust in them and turn the people back toward Egypt (Deut 17:16). Here, at the height of blessing, are the first faint seeds of the compromise that would later trouble Solomon's heart. The chapter is teaching us a sober lesson alongside the bright one: that great prosperity carries great danger, and that the blessings of God can become snares if we begin to trust in them rather than in Him. The wealth and horses that God gave could subtly draw the heart from dependence on God to confidence in chariots. The chapter is teaching us to receive God's abundant blessings with gratitude and watchfulness — to enjoy His gifts without letting them displace the Giver, and to beware that the very prosperity He grants does not become the ground of a self-reliance that forgets Him. Solomon began so well, seeking wisdom and worshiping with a thousand offerings; let his story warn us to guard our hearts in the day of plenty, holding fast to the LORD even when His gifts are 'as plenteous as stones.'

The chapter is teaching us a sober lesson alongside the bright one: that great prosperity carries great danger, and that the blessings of God can become snares if we begin to trust in them rather than in Him. The wealth and horses that God gave could subtly draw the heart from dependence on God to confidence in chariots. The chapter is teaching us to receive God's abundant blessings with gratitude and watchfulness — to enjoy His gifts without letting them displace the Giver, and to beware that the very prosperity He grants does not become the ground of a self-reliance that forgets Him. Solomon began so well, seeking wisdom and worshiping with a thousand offerings; let his story warn us to guard our hearts in the day of plenty, holding fast to the LORD even when His gifts are 'as plenteous as stones.'

Then Solomon came from his journey to the high place that was at Gibeon to Jerusalem, from before the tabernacle of the congregation, and reigned over Israel. And Solomon gathered chariots and horsemen: and he had a thousand and four hundred chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen, which he placed in the chariot cities, and with the king at Jerusalem. And the king made silver and gold at Jerusalem as plenteous as stones, and cedar trees made he as the sycomore trees that are in the vale for abundance. And Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt, and linen yarn: the king's merchants received the linen yarn at a price. And they brought up, and brought forth out of Egypt a chariot for six hundred shekels of silver, and an horse for an hundred and fifty: and so brought they out for all the kings of the Hittites, and for the kings of Syria, by their means. Guidance: God's promise is swiftly fulfilled: Solomon returns from Gibeon and reigns in such prosperity that he gathers great wealth — fourteen hundred chariots, twelve thousand horsemen, and 'silver and gold at Jerusalem as plenteous as stones,' cedar as common as the sycamore. The riches God promised flow in abundance. The chapter is showing us the LORD making good His word; having promised Solomon wealth and honour beyond all kings, He pours it out, so that gold becomes as common as stone in Jerusalem. The chapter is teaching us that God is faithful to His promises and able to bless beyond measure those whom He favours; what He pledges, He performs, and lavishly. Yet the discerning reader notes a quiet shadow in these verses: 'Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt,' multiplying chariots and horses — the very things the LORD had cautioned Israel's king against, lest he trust in them and turn the people back toward Egypt (Deut 17:16). Here, at the height of blessing, are the first faint seeds of the compromise that would later trouble Solomon's heart. The chapter is teaching us a sober lesson alongside the bright one: that great prosperity carries great danger, and that the blessings of God can become snares if we begin to trust in them rather than in Him. The wealth and horses that God gave could subtly draw the heart from dependence on God to confidence in chariots. The chapter is teaching us to receive God's abundant blessings with gratitude and watchfulness — to enjoy His gifts without letting them displace the Giver, and to beware that the very prosperity He grants does not become the ground of a self-reliance that forgets Him. Solomon began so well, seeking wisdom and worshiping with a thousand offerings; let his story warn us to guard our hearts in the day of plenty, holding fast to the LORD even when His gifts are 'as plenteous as stones.'
